speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape eight with Israel Kapin. Israel, we were talking about your intention to go to Palestine. Why did you not go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The war finished in '45. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as I said just a short while ago, one of my first decisions after the end of the war was to leave [NON-ENGLISH], not to try and start putting in roots there for a long-term future. I had a family who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] miraculously, the immediate family remained intact. And they needed help. They needed somebody to lift them out of-- out of Europe-- the shattered Europe of the war. In '45, or even the beginning of '46, it was no [? gainsaying ?] by anybody, although the camps were opened, and Auschwitz and Treblinka were known to the world for its autrocities and tragedies, nobody could have foreseen how long it would be that Palestine will become-- will get out of the British mandate, and if, indeed, a Jewish state will ever come into being. It was not a foregone conclusion. The betting was not that a Jewish state is going to-- to be so historically close. I couldn't foresee in the beginning of '46 and end of '45 that-- that the Palestine under the British mandate will be-- will become a Jewish state in May, '48. I couldn't foresee that. And I couldn't afford to sit there, again wasting time and years and opportunities, in a place which I didn't want to call home, waiting for just anything. I couldn't. I had to go somewhere. I couldn't go to Palestine. My brothers who were in Europe, and were moved by the underground, by the Israeli underground, the Jewish [INAUDIBLE] [? Underground ?] [INAUDIBLE] it was called, were taken. And they had an opportunity to-- to Palestine. But then the decision was for the family not to break up. And the fact that I was here became the decisive factor in the ultimate decision to come as a family, and join me in Australia. But I didn't have that option. I couldn't from Shanghai go to Palestine, because the mandate not only was in full force, but was even more-- more restricted after the war. And the claim for those who could come were those who came out of the camps, they were the first to have the right of entry for what they have suffered, and rehabilitation, and so on. I was not a case for rehabilitation. And you know that when America asked the British-- the British government to grant a 100,000 certificates to take the remnants of the camps, and to bring them to Israel, the answer was no. And that was-- that is when Truman made the decision to support the declaration of a Jewish state, because the-- the Labor government came into power immediately after the war, trouncing Churchill in the elections in '45. And Bevan, the foreign minister, took a pro-Arab-- a pro-Arab political stance because of the changed circumstances. And in England, that was, by then, bereft of-- of wealth, and potential colonies, and everything else. And they wanted-- and they wanted to ingratiate themselves with the Arab world. And so also, they played the Arab card at the expense of Jewish refugees. And there was no chance for people like myself to come there. I couldn't have a claim. I would have to stay a long way in the queue before I would be allowed by the British to come. With the birth certificates, I wouldn't have the morale right to ask for them. That is why we came-- I decided to go wherever it was. I had a choice between Chile and Australia. I had family in both. And I've made my decision that I will go to Anglo-Saxon country.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in May, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your family join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In subsequent years-- in '47, not all at once, they came in dribs and drabs. First came my father and mother with my sister, and then came two brothers, and then came the last of my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married in Australia in 9th of March, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name of your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laura Bates, her maiden name was Bates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I have two children. I've got a daughter by the name of [? Eva, ?] and a son by the name [NON-ENGLISH], both very pronounced Hebrew names, modern Hebrew names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've got three grandchildren. I've got agradd granddaughter of 15, Natalie. I've got a granddaughter of age five, Phoebe, and a grandson Theodore, age three. And that is the sum total.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, when you arrived in Australia after your journey from Shanghai, what sort of commitments did you make to yourself about your future, if any? Did you have a goal?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I am to be honest with myself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was absolutely pragmatic about it. I didn't have any preconceived goals, because there was nothing for me to plan on. I came to Shanghai with borrowed $500 that a friend of mine in Shanghai insisted I take from him, that I have something to untie my hands with if I want to do something. That was the sum total of my wealth, somebody else's $500. And when you've got nothing, you can't plan with nothing. There was only one thing that I knew for sure. I had resolved to rebuild my life independently [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] as a free agent, not as a man on a job. That's the only thing that I knew in my own mind, that-- that age of 27, with the experience of the war years, which would equal another person's experience during your lifetime, I-- that much I knew, that I would like to build my life. And I would like to build it as-- from scratch myself, with my own hands, my own abilities. Whatever the opportunities will be will come my way or otherwise. I had no guarantees. And I was prepared to take the risk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go into business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went into business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of business did you conduct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started by buying and selling clothing, anything that was available that one could lay hands on, to take from the factory to the store. And when I said to [? miles, ?] that I've got [? neat wear ?] for sale, he used to leave his customers and everything else, that come running-- running to Oxford chambers, where the Eagle-- Eagle Insurance company stands today in [INAUDIBLE] Street. Come in, have a look. They said, send in the lot. That was the way we used to do business in those days. It was a long time until the skills changed the other way. You used to stand, cap in hand, waiting for the [? miles ?] buyer to give you the nod, or to want to talk to you. It was-- it was a seller's market, and not a buyer's market, in 1946-47. And that this is how I started, until the family came out. And they bought some old secondhand machines. And we have put those machines to use. And we started producing on a very small scale. And it developed. The selling of it was not difficult. The problem was in those days to get raw material. The spinning mills weren't working yet. And those who were working had established customers. They didn't wait for Israel Kipen to-- and his custom that he could buy a bit of yarn. So he couldn't get-- you couldn't get raw material for anything. That was the greatest achievement, is to find raw material of any sort to be able to create-- to produce. And my break came in business in '49. I was, by then, selling-- I had a man in Sydney who used to buy from me a great-- a sizable part of my production. He was a wholesaler. I didn't sell direct to the shops. He was a wholesaler. I made a few [? bob ?] out of it. And in 1949 was the coal strike in New South Wales, which prompted the Labor government under [? Chiefney ?] to send in the troops in the mines, because it was a communist-inspired strike, which aimed at strangulating the Australian economy. And there's no better way of doing so but declaring a strike on the mines, which is followed by the railways. And in no time, the economy is strangulated. And a labor government was forced to send in the troops to break the strike. Or otherwise, the post-war Australian development would have been jeopardized to an unimaginable extent. In May, at the end of the winter season, one day I got a notice from Sydney, from that wholesaler of mine, Mr. Mintz, that he was unable to sell the goods that he has got for me. And he's sending me back 2,000 ladies cardigans. It was the end of the season. And 2,000 ladies cardigans for me in those days was more than I possessed. And it was-- it arrived smack bang right in front of me at the end of the season. And there would have been no buyers. It was too late for the season to distribute it. And I was facing bankruptcy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I've decided to go and offer it to [? GJ Kohls, ?] a parcel. A made an appointment. I went and saw the buyer, Mr. Watson, a middle-aged man, grey, middle-aged man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He looked at the sample. It was first class merchandise. He tried it, took it inside, came back, no, we're not interested. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I took back-- I took the pieces, the garment, wrapped it up, put it in a piece of paper, and opened the door, said goodbye. Wait a minute, he said, come in, will you? Give me the parcel again.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took it back for me a second time, went inside, came out with an order, send in the goods. And I sold him the entire parcel. And I got the money in seven days, 5% discount, got my money. And I was liquid again. I [? could ?] [? stop. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they have sold it like hotcakes. They'd never got such merchandise, because they used to get leftovers-- nobody gave them regular goods in those days. Came the next season, they asked for samples. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I gave them a sample. And they gave me an opening order, not a big one, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to be ready to be delivered in January some time. And I came home. I made my decision on the spot. I'm going to produce that item as much as I can. As much as I can get raw material to produce it, I will produce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I send in the first 500 garments that she asked for, whatever it is, the buyer. Within a few days, she's back, unannounced. Can we have another 500 garments? Yes, we can. When do you want it? Now. You will have it tomorrow. True? Yes, tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, that became [? apparent, and ?] few days, she used to come asking for another parcel of goods. And she got it, until one day she exhausted all my stock. And we did what we could during that season. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The next season, she asked again for a simple. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came-- I came to see her after a while. And she gave me an order for 27,000 pounds, in those days, an opening order, an opening order. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I stood there speechless. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How can I produce thousands, and thousands, and thousands, and thousands of garments? That was-- the value of a garment was 27 schillings, or whatever it is, 28 schillings. Who is going to give me so much yarn on credit? I haven't got money to buy it. Or who's going to produce it for me? I went to a spinning mill, and put this [INAUDIBLE], the famous [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: You buy hand knit yarn, to this day. I put the order on the table. I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here's my story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've got an opening order of so much. And that's only the opening orders. Going by the experience of last year, there will be much more than the opening order. I have to produce that opening order to be ready for distribution beginning of January. I have got no money to pay you for the yarn. But if you will be prepared to wait with payment until I send out the first goods and get my money in seven days, you will be paid by the end of January. And from then on, I will be able to [INAUDIBLE] order. They didn't give an answer on the spot. But they have discussed it in the board. And the answer was, yes, we will supply you with yarn. When I got that almost from them, I went to the bank. And I said to the banker, here you are. Here is the order. Here is [? Lincoln's ?] preparedness to give me the raw material. I need money for wages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you prepared to give me cash flow so I can pay wages until the end of January? Then we are in business. The answer was yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had to go and start bringing together disparate [INAUDIBLE] in different corners of town, three machines, four machines, five machines standing there, and coordinated with finishing, coordinating with [INAUDIBLE], coordinating with that. Anyhow, a long story short, it was produced. It was delivered. And it was paid for. And we entered a relationship with [? Kohl's, ?] which lasted for 10 years. I became the number one supplier of ladies knitwear to [INAUDIBLE] Australia, not [INAUDIBLE] Melbourne, [INAUDIBLE] Australia, and got a letter-- a document of commendation for services rendered to the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in business till?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in business for 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished business in '77. But I finished business with them after 10 years, because after 10 years, things have changed. They didn't want to go with the times. I wanted to buy [? fully ?] [? fashioned ?] machines when that came into-- and they didn't want to. They said, your product as it is is good enough for me. I knew that they are looking backwards and not forwards. So I branched out on my own apart from the big-- I finished up in one year deliver to [? Kohl's ?] 200,000 garments in one season.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, in 1977--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What decisions had you come to about the direction of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1977, I was 57 years old. I had 30 years of business. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And during the business years, some people said that with all that, it was only a sideline with me. My real business was in communal affairs. The in-between, I used to attend to my own business. That was the main thrust of my interest intellectually and communally. I had always had a sense that what I want to do with my life is more than provide for myself, or provide for my family, and ensure they are well-being. I thought that if life-- my life has to have any sense, it has to provide more than just for myself. It has to spread, to widen, the framework of my-- of my achievement, or creativity, or whatever it is, especially when the needs were crying out in the community that had so many things to be established, and to be done, and to be achieved there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- and that is-- that is what drove me. That drove me, first of all, my involvement with the Zionist movement. I was a president of the Victorian Zionist Organization almost shortly after my arrival.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] for almost 10 years, in '59, I took over the presidency of the State Zionist Council of Victoria, stayed there for a very short time because that was not my cup of tea. I was sick and tired of seeing two or three pictures every Friday in the paper of myself. And that was not what I intended, to be the-- the wages of my communal activity. I didn't want pictures. I wanted some tangible results and achievements, rather than pictures. And during my time as president of the State Zionist Council, and because I was the president of the Victorian Zionist Organization before that, who owned the [? Bialik ?] Afternoon School was the possession of the Victorian Zionist Organization. It was the pilot enterprise. It belonged to the Victorian Zionist Organization. The building [INAUDIBLE] belonged to Victorian Zionist Organization. So when I was the president of the State Zionist Council, I pushed through a resolution in the State Zionist Council that-- that we should convert the [? Bialik ?] Sunday School to a day school. And I was instrumental in converting the ownership of the property that we had in [? Carlton ?] to the new enterprise of [? Bialik ?] Day School, which was under the auspices of the State Zionist Council. And at the same time, [INAUDIBLE] was-- [INAUDIBLE] was involved with the Victorian Zionist Organization in the '50s, the whole of the '50s, I was a member of the executive of the Mount Scopus College from 1950 to 1959, struggling to give a face, and a content, and a direction to that school, because Mount Scopus College, at its initial stages, was directionless, because the people-- the only men who knew something what he wanted was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Hebrew-speaking man who came from Israel here. The others, who were local people, who were the best of intention, they've never known what a Jewish day school is, and never knew what the contents of such a school-- what is the intellectual baggage of such an enterprise? What should be the direction? All they knew that Judaism means religion. What I was going to ask you is when you actually were-- this is all very much part of your life. Did you and embark on a more formal sort of education for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not until I finished with all those businesses. After I finished 10 years of Scopus, and put them on the rails to the extent to which it was possible at that time to put them-- and I was the one who has forced them to adopt a formal curriculum in Hebrew studies other than a prayer book and the Bible, because the entire notion of Jewish Studies as a cultural pursuit of Jewish history, and so on, and so forth, and Jewish language, and Hebrew language, and so on, was totally misunderstood, because for them, the prayer book was the textbook. And the Bible was the history book. See? And they didn't even understand what I was aiming at. They couldn't even understand what they were aiming at. And then, when I've-- when we have converted-- when I left them in '59, and I spent two years at the State Zionist Council as its head, and then in '63 we opened [? Bialik ?] as a day school. And within a couple of years, we have sent our children on the Independence Day onto the stage reciting in Hebrew, and speaking fluently in Hebrew, and at the same time doing all the other things that they did among [? Scopus justice ?] as easily as [? justice ?] as at ease and so on, I have forced Mount Scopus to reassess their concept of what the Jewish education is, because I've proved to them that it can be a wider framework. The Jewish education is not a narrow straight jacket, which is beginning and its end in the framework of Jewish religious observance, and-- and-- and a matter of [HEBREW], and a matter of grace after meals, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to talk about your own personal career after you finished your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I finished my business, and I finished it because objective circumstances were such that I could see that an industry which Jewish refugees who arrived here in the early '50s built, because knitting industry was nonexistent before the war, with very few exceptions. Australia imported knit wear from England. They didn't have much of an industry of their own. Jewish people who came after the war created this industry. Then when Hong Kong and Taiwan began exporting on a world scale, they have challenged that industry. And it was obvious to anyone who could see the writing on the wall that this industry will not survive if the degree of imports that the government is deliberately bringing in into the country is going to continue, and the industry would have to fold.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you make the decision to go back and pursue a different career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've made the decision-- I've made the decision to-- to wind up my business in '75. And it took me two years to get out of the cycle of business, because you are always a year in advance with the winter season, and then with the summer season, until you unbound all that, until you came through with the production, and with the distribution, and so on, and so on. It was in 1977 that I finally have shaken off and closed the chapter of 30 years of business activity in Australia. And I was 57 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Israel. We'll end tape eight now.
